title: Molecular relapse of B-ALL with CD19/CD22-negative AUL-like phenotype in a 44-year-old male after alloSCT and CD19 CAR-T

patient.chief_complaint: Scheduled follow-up after alloSCT and CD19 CAR-T for B-ALL, with interest in outpatient treatment options
patient.vital_signs: Temp 36.9 °C, BP not reported at this visit, HR not reported at this visit, RR not reported at this visit, SpO₂ not reported at this visit.

1.setting: Outpatient hematology/oncology follow-up after alloSCT and CAR-T
1.capabilities: You are evaluating a clinically stable patient in a routine follow-up setting after intensive leukemia therapy. You can review prior history and recent clinical course, perform a targeted examination, and request standard follow-up evaluations appropriate to an ambulatory visit. Decisions focus on surveillance and whether further workup for relapse or complications is indicated.

2.setting: Hematology follow-up with bone marrow reassessment
2.capabilities: The patient is undergoing more detailed evaluation because of his leukemia history and evolving laboratory findings. You can organize marrow-based assessments and additional consultations to clarify disease status. Some specialized tests are still pending.

3.setting: Relapse and transplant planning conference in a heavily pretreated leukemia patient
3.capabilities: You now have results from bone marrow morphology, flow cytometry, clonality assays, and broad molecular profiling together with a comprehensive treatment history including alloSCT, bispecifics, inotuzumab, blinatumomab, and CD19 CAR-T. You are expected to interpret the pattern of MRD relapse, phenotypic evolution, and donor-derived CHIP, and to formulate a realistic outpatient-oriented salvage and transplant-bridging strategy.

demographics.valid_request_if: Requests for demographic background, performance status, comorbidities, social situation, or lifestyle context.
demographics.content:
Age: 44 years
Sex: Male
Performance status: ECOG 0
Comorbidities: Arterial hypertension, hyperlipidemia, mild glucose intolerance. History of acute leukemia and allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
Social history: Married, living with wife and 16-year-old daughter. Has three siblings; both parents are deceased. History of prior drug abuse. The patient maintains unusual lifestyle habits, including waking daily at 02:30 a.m. for cold-water swimming. Diet consists of multiple protein supplements (eight types) and approximately 15 hard-boiled eggs per week. Known for refusing inpatient treatment.
Height: 181 cm; Weight: 84 kg

history_physical.valid_request_if: Requests for interval history, current symptoms, medication adherence, or physical examination summary.
history_physical.content:
2024-07-04: The patient presented for scheduled follow-up, reporting stable overall condition. He denied fever, night sweats, or weight loss. No exertional dyspnea, abdominal pain, or neurological complaints were reported. The patient informed the team that he had stopped some medications. He actively inquired about “next treatment options,” emphasizing his preference for outpatient-based regimens.
Current medication: aciclovir 400 mg 1-0-1, posaconazole stopped, 1-0-0, cotrimoxazole 960 mg three times per week (taken irregularly), bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1-0-1, ramipril 5 mg 1-0-1, amlodipine 5 mg 1-0-1, atorvastatin 10 mg (stopped), folic acid 5 mg 1-0-0, magnesium 300 mg 1-0-0, and various nutritional supplements.

cbc_chemistry.valid_request_if: Requests for blood counts, routine chemistry, inflammatory markers, or cardiac biomarkers.
cbc_chemistry.content:
Hb 10.6 g/dL, WBC 3.1 × 10⁹/L, Plt 98 × 10⁹/L; MCV 92 fL, MCH 29 pg.
Differential: neutrophils 42%, lymphocytes 48%, monocytes 7%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 1%.
Coagulation: INR 1.1, aPTT 30 sec.
Chemistry: Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.5 mmol/L, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, BUN 19 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, LDH 220 U/L, uric acid 5.2 mg/dL, CRP 0.6 mg/dL.
Cardiac biomarkers: NT-proBNP 460 pg/mL, troponin T 0.012 ng/mL.

baseline_imaging.valid_request_if: Requests for recent ultrasound, echocardiography, or sinus imaging.
baseline_imaging.content:
2024-07-09: Abdominal ultrasound showed normal liver and spleen size, no lymphadenopathy, and no ascites.
Echocardiography demonstrated preserved left ventricular function (LVEF 62%), without pericardial effusion.
Sinus CT revealed bilateral maxillary sinus opacification with suspicion of fungal superinfection.

bone_marrow.valid_request_if: Requests for bone marrow morphology, cellularity, dysplasia, and flow cytometry.
bone_marrow.content:
Bone marrow biopsy (2024-07-09): The marrow was hypocellular (cellularity 25%) with therapy-related toxic changes. Trilineage hematopoiesis was present but displayed dysplastic features: megakaryopoiesis was reduced with small, hypolobulated forms; erythropoiesis showed megaloblastoid maturation; and granulopoiesis exhibited toxic granulation and nuclear irregularities. There was no blast excess (<5% by morphology).
Flow cytometry revealed a normal distribution of myeloid and lymphoid populations with T-cells showing mild inversion of the CD4/CD8 ratio. A small blast population (0.1%) was detected, characterized by CD19-/CD22-, CD34^dim, CD33-, CD10-, CD20-, CD117+, CD5+, CD7+, CD56-, cyMPO-, cyTdT-, cyCD3-.

cytogenetics_chimerism.valid_request_if: Requests for karyotype or post-transplant chimerism status.

extended_diagnostics.valid_request_if: Requests for ENT procedures, infection workup, pulmonary function, CSF analysis, or PET-CT findings.
extended_diagnostics.content:
ENT consultation (2024-07-13) with endoscopic sinus surgery revealed a fungal mass, which was excised completely. Culture grew Aspergillus fumigatus (azole-sensitive). Histology showed typical hyphae. Following infectious disease consultation, antifungal therapy was switched to isavuconazole for outpatient use, and liposomal amphotericin B was reserved for inpatient administration.
Pulmonary function testing showed a mildly reduced DLCO (65% predicted).
Blood cultures were negative.
Viral PCR monitoring: CMV, EBV, and adenovirus all negative.
Serology: HBV, HCV, and HIV negative.
Immunologic profile: CD4⁺ 110/µL, CD8⁺ 320/µL (ratio 0.34); IgG 220 mg/dL.
LP was performed: cerebrospinal fluid was clear and colorless. Cell count 2/µL; protein 42 mg/dL; glucose 68 mg/dL. Cytospin and morphology showed no blasts.
FDG-PET/CT (2024-07-11) demonstrated no hypermetabolic lymphadenopathy or extranodal disease. There was intense FDG uptake in both maxillary sinuses. Previously noted abdominal subcutaneous nodules had resolved. No abnormal uptake was observed in the liver, spleen, bone marrow, or CNS. Mild symmetrical uptake in paraspinal and thigh muscles was attributed to recent physical activity.

molecular_panel.valid_request_if: Requests for leukemia-associated mutation profile, clonality assays, and donor-related findings.
molecular_panel.content:
No changes were detected in TP53, NPM1, FLT3 (including ITD and TKD variants), IDH1, IDH2, DNMT3A, ASXL1, RUNX1, CEBPA, NRAS, KRAS, KIT, WT1, PHF6, BCOR, BCORL1, PTPN11, EZH2, STAG2, SRSF2, SF3B1, U2AF1, ZRSR2, or KMT2A. No BCR::ABL1 fusion.
A single TET2 mutation (VAF 12%) was identified, which was also detectable in the donor cell product, most consistent with clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) from donor origin rather than disease-related.
Clonal rearrangements involving IGK V1-27 KDE and IGH V3-74 D3-9 J6 were detected at a sensitivity level of 1 × 10⁻³.

mrd_status.valid_request_if: Requests for measurable residual disease status, level, and immunophenotypic features.
mrd_status.content: MRD positive (0.1%). Immunophenotypic evolution was suspected, with loss of CD19 and CD22 expression indicating a phenotypic switch compared to the diagnostic profile.

treatment_history.valid_request_if: Requests for a detailed overview of prior leukemia treatments, alloSCT, DLI, bispecific antibodies, inotuzumab, blinatumomab, and CD19 CAR-T.
treatment_history.content:
Diagnosis (2020-09-15): Common B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL), Philadelphia chromosome–negative, CD20 expression 74%. Bone marrow showed 96% blasts, peripheral blood 75% blasts. No CNS involvement. Leukocytes: 21 × 10⁹/L at diagnosis.
Induction therapy (2020-09-22): Rituximab 375 mg/m² (day 1), intrathecal methotrexate 15 mg, vincristine 2 mg absolute, daunorubicin 45 mg/m², PEG-asparaginase 2000 U/m², and dexamethasone 10 mg/m².
Bone marrow on day 22 showed persistent blasts.
Induction II (2020-10-20): Rituximab 375 mg/m²; intrathecal methotrexate 15 mg; cyclophosphamide 1000 mg/m²; cytarabine 75 mg/m²; PEG-asparaginase 1000 U/m² (reduced for hepatic steatosis); and 6-mercaptopurine 60 mg/m² (interrupted for hyperbilirubinemia).
Consolidation I (2020-11-22): Rituximab 375 mg/m² day 60; dexamethasone 10 mg/m² days 61–65; vindesine 3 mg/m² (max. 5 mg) day 61; methotrexate 1500 mg/m² day 1; etoposide phosphate 250 mg/m² days 4–5; cytarabine 2000 mg/m² ×2 day 5; intrathecal triple prophylaxis (Ara-C/MTX/dexa) day 12.
Consolidation II (2020-12-20): Rituximab 375 mg/m² day 0; methotrexate 1500 mg/m² days 1 and 15; 6-mercaptopurine 25 mg/m² days 1–7 and 15–21; PEG-asparaginase 1000 U/m² days 2 and 16; triple intrathecal prophylaxis days 1 and 15.
Re-induction: Prednisolone 3 × 20 mg/m² days 1–14; vindesine 3 mg/m² days 1 and 7; doxorubicin 25 mg/m² days 1 and 7; PEG-asparaginase 1000 U/m² day 14.
Relapse (2021-02-11): Switched to blinatumomab, achieving molecular CR after cycle 1. Subsequent cycles: 2021-03-09 and 2021-03-23.
Allogeneic SCT (2021-04-22): HLA-identical unrelated donor; conditioning with TBI (12 Gy), cyclophosphamide, and ATG. Complications: symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection (Delta), sepsis, severe mucositis, transplant-associated TMA, and PRES. Cyclosporine A discontinued. MRI showed cortical and subcortical FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with PRES. Treated with prednisolone 2 mg/kg, gradual taper, and antihypertensives. 2021-05-22: Achieved molecular CR with full donor chimerism.
Molecular relapse (2022-02-15): MRD positivity (0.4%) and 4.8% recipient chimerism. Blinatumomab restarted (2022-03-12) at 28 µg/d, dose-reduced to 9 µg/d for CTCAE grade III fever (40.5 °C), later re-started. EBV reactivation treated with rituximab 375 mg/m² on 2022-04-05 and 2022-04-14. Donor lymphocyte infusions with 1 × 10⁶ CD3+ cells/kg on 06/02/22, DLI 2 with 5 × 10⁶ on 07/03/22, and DLI 3 with 1 × 10⁷ on 08/05/22. This was followed by severe gastrointestinal GVHD, histologically grade III, overall grade IV. GvHD was treated with prednisolone 2 mg/kg, ruxolitinib 10 mg BID, ECP (from 2022-10-02), and etanercept (from 2022-10-12): achieved CR, maintained on low-dose steroids and ruxolitinib.
Relapse occurred on 2022-12-28: The patient developed new palpable abdominal subcutaneous nodules. MRD positivity with 2.1% recipient chimerism. Skin biopsy confirmed leukemic infiltration (CD19⁺, CD22⁺).
Bridging therapy: Inotuzumab ozogamicin: cycle 1 (2023-01-27, 0.8 mg/m²), 2023-02-04 (0.5 mg/m²), 2023-02-11 (0.5 mg/m²).
CAR-T therapy (2023-03-07): Tisagenlecleucel infusion after fludarabine 30 mg/m²/day and cyclophosphamide 500 mg/m²/day (days –5 to –3). Developed grade 2 CRS (fever, hypotension) treated with tocilizumab ×4. Neutropenic fever (2023-03-13): Staphylococcus hominis (CVC) and Staphylococcus epidermidis (port); port explanted. Treated with piperacillin/tazobactam followed by meropenem and vancomycin. The patient achieved molecular and metabolic CR (FDG-PET negative).
2023-09-01: Prolonged cytopenia: CD34⁺ stem cell boost from alloSCT donor (2.43 × 10⁶ CD34⁺/kg).
2023-12-15: Bone marrow MRD 0.01%.
2024-01-15: Bone marrow MRD 0.02%.
2024-02-08: Inotuzumab ozogamicin re-initiated → molecular CR with full chimerism after cycle 2.

disease_status.valid_request_if: Requests for a concise description of the current relapse pattern and immunophenotype.
disease_status.content: Molecular relapse of ALL, post–alloSCT, post–blinatumomab, inotuzumab, and CD19-CAR-T, with clonal IGH/IGK rearrangements (MRD 0.1%). Flow cytometry suggested phenotypic switch with loss of CD19/CD22 and emergence of an acute undifferentiated leukemia–like immunophenotype.

special_considerations.valid_request_if: Requests for contextual details affecting treatment choice, including performance status, prior toxicities, and social factors.
special_considerations.content:
2025-02: The patient presented with MRD relapse in the setting of extensive prior therapy including alloSCT, inotuzumab, blinatumomab, and CD19-CAR-T. Bone marrow reserve was fragile with therapy-related dysplasia, but organ function remained preserved. Performance status was ECOG 0.
Socially, the patient maintained a highly active lifestyle and strong family engagement, but expressed reluctance toward prolonged inpatient treatment. Given the history of severe GVHD and infectious complications, immune escape was considered a plausible relapse mechanism.

round_1.working_diagnosis: Acute leukemia in remission post allogeneic HCT with suspected sinusitis

treatment_plan.action: Order detailed laboratory workup including CBC with differential, inflammatory markers, chimerism, lymphocyte subpopulations, and CMV/EBV PCR to assess disease and immune status.
Obtain ENT consultation for evaluation of suspected fungal superinfection of the sinuses.
Schedule bone marrow biopsy for cytology, immunophenotyping, and chimerism to evaluate for molecular relapse.
Inquire about reasons for medication discontinuation and counsel on the importance of antiviral, antifungal, and PJP prophylaxis in the immunocompromised setting.
Advise patient to reduce or stop uncontrolled nutritional supplementation given elevated creatinine.

key_findings_quotes: The patient presented for scheduled follow-up, reporting stable overall condition. He denied fever, night sweats, or weight loss.
The patient informed the team that he had stopped some medications.
He actively inquired about 'next treatment options,' emphasizing his preference for outpatient-based regimens.
Sinus CT revealed bilateral maxillary sinus opacification with suspicion of fungal superinfection.
History of acute leukemia and post alloSCT.

round_2.working_diagnosis: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia in cytological remission post allogeneic HCT with possible molecular relapse; Aspergillus sinusitis
differentials: Molecular relapse of ALL with phenotypic evolution

treatment_plan.action: Initiate azole antifungal therapy (isavuconazole) for confirmed Aspergillus fumigatus infection.
Administer IVIG substitution for severe hypogammaglobulinemia (IgG 220 mg/dL).
Order molecular genetics panel on bone marrow to further characterize the small CD19-/CD22- blast population with aberrant CD117+/CD5+/CD7+ phenotype.
Interpret flow cytometry findings in context of prior CD19+/CD22+ B-ALL to assess for phenotypic switch and lineage ambiguity.

key_findings_quotes: The marrow was hypocellular (cellularity 25%) with therapy-related toxic changes. Trilineage hematopoiesis was present but displayed dysplastic features.
There was no blast excess (<5% by morphology).
A small blast population (0.1%) was detected, characterized by CD19-/CD22-, CD34^dim, CD33-, CD10-, CD20-, CD117+, CD5+, CD7+, CD56-, cyMPO-, cyTdT-, cyCD3-.
Immunologic profile: CD4+ 110/uL, CD8+ 320/uL (ratio 0.34); IgG 220 mg/dL.

round_3.working_diagnosis: Molecular relapse of B-lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma NOS (WHO 2022) with antigen-loss (CD19-/CD22-) and phenotypic switch toward AUL-like profile
differentials: 5th molecular relapse of ALL with immune escape

treatment_plan.action: Start outpatient salvage therapy with azacitidine and venetoclax as bridging strategy to second allogeneic stem cell transplantation.
Initiate HLA typing of the patient's three siblings for haploidentical donor search in preparation for second allogeneic transplantation.
Evaluate clinical trial options and initiate molecular tumor board discussion for off-label therapy alternatives.
Continue antifungal and antiviral prophylaxis with close infection monitoring during salvage therapy.
Involve palliative care team given heavily pretreated status, patient preferences, and uncertain prognosis.
Favor outpatient administration and minimize hospital stays in line with patient's strong preference against prolonged inpatient treatment.

key_findings_quotes: MRD positive (0.1%). Immunophenotypic evolution was suspected, with loss of CD19 and CD22 expression indicating a phenotypic switch compared to the diagnostic profile.
No changes were detected in TP53, NPM1, FLT3... No BCR::ABL1 fusion. No targetable mutations.
Clonal rearrangements involving IGK V1-27 KDE and IGH V3-74 D3-9 J6 were detected at a sensitivity level of 1x10^-3.

Given the molecular relapse of B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma, NOS (WHO 2022), with antigen-loss (CD19-/CD22-) and phenotypic switch toward an AUL-like profile, the patient will be started on outpatient salvage therapy with azacitidine and venetoclax.

Therapy is intended as a bridging strategy in preparation for a second allogeneic stem cell transplantation. Haploidentical transplantation is preferred, and HLA typing of the patient's three siblings has been initiated.

The patient maintained a highly active lifestyle and strong family engagement, but expressed reluctance toward prolonged inpatient treatment. Given the history of severe GVHD and infectious complications, immune escape was considered a plausible relapse mechanism.
